title: Oily skin and large pores
excerpt: Ayurveda links oily skin and large pores to an imbalance of Kapha and Pitta doshas, and the classical texts offer a surprisingly practical daily routine to bring your complexion back to its natural clarity.
at_a_glance: Oily skin in Ayurveda points to excess Kapha and Pitta doshas affecting the fatty tissue layer, not just surface grease.
Light, bitter and astringent foods and Multani mitti cleansing are the classical first-line tools for balancing oily skin.

These suggestions are general guidance: a qualified vaidya or dermatologist can assess your constitution and give a personalised plan, especially if oily skin comes with cysts, hormonal changes or persistent breakouts.

body.heading: why your face feels greasy by noon
paragraphs: If you find yourself blotting your face before lunch, or noticing that your pores look noticeably larger in the mirror after a humid afternoon, you are not alone. Oily skin is one of the most common skin complaints in India, and the classical Ayurvedic texts treated it as a condition with a root cause, not just a cosmetic nuisance.

In Ayurveda the quality is called Snigdha tvak, meaning unctuous or greasy skin. The texts describe it as a sign that Kapha dosha, which governs lubrication, cohesion and heaviness in the body, has accumulated in the skin tissues along with an aggravated Pitta. Pitta brings heat and inflammation, while Kapha brings excess secretion. The combination produces skin that is oily, prone to clogged pores, and sometimes prone to small eruptions or a dull complexion (Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana).

The sebaceous glands embedded in the skin's dermis are understood in Ayurveda as part of the Meda dhatu, the fatty tissue layer. When Meda dhatu becomes excessive or poorly transformed, its excess finds expression at the skin surface as sebum. Diet, climate, sleep patterns and stress all influence how much Meda accumulates and how well it is metabolised.

body.heading: what aggravates oily skin
paragraphs: The Bhavaprakasha, a later but widely used classical reference, lists heavy, unctuous foods, excessive sweet and salty tastes, daytime sleeping, and a sedentary lifestyle as factors that increase Kapha and Meda in the body (Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda). These are exactly the habits that many of us slip into: late nights followed by long lie-ins, frequent fried or processed snacks, and hours at a desk.

Pitta is worsened by spicy food, excess sun exposure, alcohol and anger. When both doshas are already slightly elevated, as they commonly are in people with a Kapha-Pitta constitution, even small excesses can tip the skin into overproduction. The Sushruta Samhita also notes that improperly digested food generates Ama, a metabolic residue, that circulates and can manifest in the skin as a dull, congested quality (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana).

Seasonal changes matter too. The monsoon and early autumn months tend to aggravate both Vata and Pitta simultaneously, while late winter and spring are peak Kapha seasons. Many people with oily skin notice that their skin is at its worst between February and May, which maps to the classical Kapha accumulation period.

body.heading: a grounded daily routine for oily skin
paragraphs: The classical approach is not to strip the skin of all oil, which only prompts more secretion, but to lighten the diet, support agni (the digestive fire), and use Ruksha (drying) and Tikta (bitter) qualities topically and internally. The Ashtanga Hridaya recommends Ruksha (rough/dry), Laghu (light) and Tikta (bitter) therapies for conditions of Kapha excess (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

For cleansing, Multani mitti (Fuller's earth) has been used for generations in Indian households and fits perfectly into this framework: it has absorbing, cooling and lightening properties that counteract both Kapha and Pitta. A simple face mask of Multani mitti mixed with rose water, applied once or twice a week and rinsed off once it is almost dry but not completely hard, can visibly reduce the greasy feeling. Chandan (sandalwood) powder mixed with the same rose water is a gentler daily option for very sensitive skin, as sandalwood has both cooling and mildly astringent properties acknowledged in the Bhavaprakasha Nighantu.

Internally, keeping the diet light is equally important. Reduce or avoid heavy fried food, excess dairy, cold drinks and sweets. Favour warm cooked food that is easy to digest: simple dals, steamed vegetables, whole grains. Including bitter and astringent tastes, through vegetables like karela (bitter gourd), methi (fenugreek), and herbs like neem, helps counteract Kapha at its root. Staying well hydrated with warm water through the day supports the body in metabolising Meda more efficiently.

Triphala, the classical three-fruit formulation described extensively in the Ashtanga Hridaya and elsewhere, taken at night with warm water, gently supports digestion and the skin simultaneously (Ashtanga Hridaya, Chikitsasthana). It is one of the most widely recommended general-purpose formulations in Ayurveda precisely because it works across all three doshas. Check with a vaidya before starting any formulation, especially if you take other medications.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: The suggestions above are general information and are not a substitute for a diagnosis or a personalised treatment plan from a qualified physician or registered Ayurvedic vaidya.

See a doctor promptly if your oily skin is accompanied by severe or rapidly spreading acne, painful cysts, or nodules under the skin. See a dermatologist if over-the-counter or home approaches have not helped after several weeks, or if your skin suddenly becomes much oilier than usual without an obvious lifestyle reason, as this can sometimes be linked to hormonal conditions such as polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) or thyroid changes. Redness, swelling or skin that is sore to the touch also warrants a professional opinion. An Ayurvedic vaidya can assess your specific constitution and current imbalance and offer a tailored programme that goes beyond general guidance.

try_this_today.title: Multani mitti weekly mask
try_this_today.description: Mix one tablespoon of Multani mitti with enough rose water to form a smooth paste. Apply to a clean face, leave on until almost dry (not cracking), then rinse gently with cool water. Once or twice a week is enough.

try_this_today.title: Warm water through the day
try_this_today.description: Replace cold or room-temperature water with warm water sipped regularly. This simple habit supports Agni and helps the body metabolise Meda more efficiently, which over weeks can reduce excess oiliness.

try_this_today.title: Bitter vegetables at one meal daily
try_this_today.description: Include karela, methi leaves or neem-based chutneys at least once a day. Bitter taste (Tikta rasa) directly counteracts Kapha accumulation according to classical Ayurvedic dietary principles.

try_this_today.title: Triphala at bedtime
try_this_today.description: Half a teaspoon of Triphala churna stirred into a glass of warm water before bed gently aids digestion and supports skin clarity. Consult a vaidya before starting if you are pregnant or on other medications.

try_this_today.title: Avoid daytime sleeping
try_this_today.description: Divasvapna (daytime sleep) is listed in classical texts as a Kapha-aggravating habit. If you feel drowsy after lunch, a 15-minute rest is fine, but long afternoon naps increase Meda and can worsen oily skin over time.

words_to_know.definition: Unctuous or greasy skin; the Ayurvedic term for oily skin quality.
One of the three doshas; governs lubrication, cohesion and heaviness in the body.
One of the three doshas; governs heat, metabolism and transformation.
The fatty tissue layer in the body; excess or poor-quality Meda manifests as oily skin.
Metabolic residue from improperly digested food; circulates and can contribute to skin congestion.
The quality of dryness or roughness; therapeutically used to reduce Kapha excess.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana · Caraka Samhita, Vimanasthana · Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana · Ashtanga Hridaya, Chikitsasthana
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
